verses.commentary: Six historical markers locate the start of John's ministry: Tiberius Caesar (year 15 = AD 28-29), Pilate (governor of Judea), Herod Antipas (Galilee), Philip (Iturea, Trachonitis), Lysanias (Abilene). Luke writes as historian.

During the high priesthood of Annas and Caiaphas (technically Annas had been deposed but retained influence; Caiaphas was his son-in-law). 'The word of God came to John' — prophetic call formula (Jeremiah 1:1). John takes the prophet role.
Went through all the region around the Jordan, proclaiming a baptism of repentance for the forgiveness of sins. Mobile mission, anchored in repentance and forgiveness — the John-Jesus continuity.
Isaiah 40:3-5 quoted (Luke quotes more than Matthew/Mark). 'Prepare the way of the Lord, make his paths straight.' Wilderness voice readying the highway.
'Every valley filled, every mountain leveled, crooked made straight, rough places smooth.' Topographical leveling as image of social and moral preparation.
'All flesh (pasa sarx, πᾶσα σάρξ) shall see the salvation of God.' Universal scope — characteristic Lukan extension of the quotation through verse 6. The Gentile horizon already in view.
'Brood of vipers (gennemata echidnon, γεννήματα ἐχιδνῶν) — who warned you to flee from the coming wrath?' Strong prophetic rebuke to the crowds. Echo of Genesis 3 imagery.
'Bear fruits worthy of repentance. Do not begin to say, We have Abraham as our father. From these stones God can raise up children to Abraham.' Same as Matthew 3:9. Descent cannot replace repentance.
'Axe is laid to the root.' Judgment imminent. Trees without fruit are cut and burned.
Crowds: 'What then shall we do?' Practical follow-up question. Lukan distinctive — ethical specifics by category.
'Whoever has two tunics, share with whoever has none; whoever has food, do likewise.' Concrete generosity, not abstract piety. Common ethic for the crowds.
Tax collectors also come: 'Teacher, what shall we do?' Even collaborator-class repentance is welcomed. Specific instructions follow.
'Collect no more than what is appointed.' Not abandon the job — practice the job justly. Reform within vocation.

Soldiers (Roman auxiliaries or Herodian troops): 'And we, what shall we do?' Three rules: no intimidation/extortion, no false accusation, content with wages. Even military service can be sanctified through ethical practice.
People in expectation (prosdokontos, προσδοκῶντος), wondering whether John might be the Messiah. Anticipation crystallizes around the prophetic figure.

John answers: 'I baptize you with water; but one mightier comes — straps of whose sandals I am not worthy to untie. He will baptize you with the Holy Spirit and fire.' Mightier one's superior baptism.
Winnowing fork in his hand — clear his threshing floor, gather wheat into the barn, burn the chaff with unquenchable fire. Final separation.
With many other exhortations he preached good news (euengelizeto, εὐηγγελίζετο) to the people. Despite the severity, his message was good news.
Herod (Antipas) reproved by John for Herodias his brother's wife and other evil deeds. John's prophetic confrontation of political power.
Herod added this to all his evils: locked John in prison. Luke compresses the imprisonment here, ahead of the baptism narrative — narrative cleanup of John before Jesus' ministry.
When all the people were being baptized — Jesus also was baptized. 'And while he was praying' (Lukan distinctive — Jesus at prayer) — heaven was opened.

Holy Spirit descended on him in bodily form (somatiko eidei, σωματικῷ εἴδει) like a dove. Voice from heaven: 'You are my beloved Son; with you I am well pleased.' Trinitarian moment. Same threefold OT echo (Psalm 2, Genesis 22, Isaiah 42) as Matthew.

Jesus about thirty years old when he began his ministry. Son (as was supposed — hos enomizeto, ὡς ἐνομίζετο) of Joseph. Adoptive paternity carefully qualified. Heli — Joseph's father (possibly Joseph's father-in-law Heli, via Mary).
Genealogical descent begins — moves backward in reverse direction from Matthew. Matthat, Levi, Melki, Jannai, Joseph — non-prominent generations.
Mattathias, Amos, Nahum, Esli, Naggai. Inter-testamental period names. Pious but undistinguished.
Maath, Mattathias, Semein, Josek, Joda. Hellenistic-era names. The genealogy passes through the silent centuries.
Joanan, Rhesa, Zerubbabel, Shealtiel, Neri. Zerubbabel — post-exilic leader (Ezra 3). Pivot through the exilic generation.
Melki, Addi, Cosam, Elmadam, Er. Names mostly unknown to OT — preserved family memory.
Joshua, Eliezer, Jorim, Matthat, Levi. Names recurring in clusters reflect family naming patterns.
Simeon, Judah, Joseph, Jonam, Eliakim. Patriarchal names recur in successive generations.
Melea, Menna, Mattatha, Nathan, David. Nathan, not Solomon — Luke traces through David's other son, possibly through Mary's line.
Jesse, Obed, Boaz, Salmon, Nahshon. Ruth and Boaz line, leading back through tribe of Judah.
Amminadab, Ram, Hezron, Perez, Judah. Tribal patriarchal line.
Jacob, Isaac, Abraham, Terah, Nahor. The covenant patriarchs. Genesis 11-50 in reverse.
Serug, Reu, Peleg, Eber, Shelah. Pre-Abrahamic Semitic line — Genesis 11.
Cainan, Arphaxad, Shem, Noah, Lamech. Through the flood survivors. The Noah generation. (Cainan after Arphaxad is in the LXX but not the Masoretic text.)
Methuselah, Enoch, Jared, Mahalalel, Cainan. Pre-flood patriarchs. Enoch — who walked with God and was not (Genesis 5:24).

Enosh, Seth, Adam, the son of God (huiou theou, υἱοῦ θεοῦ). Luke's genealogy reaches all the way to Adam — and Adam is called son of God. Christological framing: Jesus is the new Adam, the true Son. Where Matthew began with Abraham (Israel-focused), Luke ends with Adam (humanity-wide). The Gentile horizon is built into the genealogy itself.
